headline: alloc + indexed-write is 2.6x SLOWER than [].push() in this hexa interp; migration reverted; baseline preserved byte-perfect.

context.predecessor: omega_audio_5 brought vocal_hexa to ~107ms via period-table; closed math gap.
context.hypothesis: Per-sample 2x .push() into byte buffer (4800 push/call for 100ms vocal) is the remaining cost; pre-alloc + indexed write should beat it.
context.prior_signal: omega_interp_3 added array_alloc(N) but vocal_hexa_alloc bench was NULL-RESULT — already a soft signal that this pattern doesn't help here.
context.scope: anima/anima-speak/anima_audio.hexa only. NO docker, NO hexa-lang, NO git changes.

before_baseline_ms.note: Run 1 — pre-migration. vocal_lf and breath were not yet migrated in either pass; their numbers reflect system noise variance only.

after_alloc_indexed_ms.note: Run 2 — vocal_hexa only migrated to array_alloc + indexed-write. vocal_lf/breath unchanged (still push); their slowdowns reflect coincident system load. chain blew up to 2382ms because it consumes vocal_hexa output and the slow vocal_hexa amplifies.

after_revert_ms.note: Run 3 — vocal_hexa reverted to push pattern. Slightly higher than run 1 due to ambient load but in the same regime; the alloc-pattern's 2.6x slowdown is reproducible signal, not noise.

vocal_hexa_delta.result: MISS by 5.7x; SLOWER by 2.6x vs starting point

byte_hash_check.interpretation: alloc+indexed-write produced byte-perfect output — semantics preserved. Failure was purely performance, not correctness. WAV header inline and indexed sample writes are functionally equivalent to _wav_header() + .push pairs.

gap_to_python_worker.delta_from_audio_5: +0.42 — system load drift between cycles, not a regression vs audio-5 (same source).

decomposition_decision.migrate_breath_hexa: SKIPPED — propagating a 2.6x-slower pattern to more code would compound the regression.
decomposition_decision.migrate_pipe_hexa: SKIPPED — same reason; chain already shows the amplification effect.
decomposition_decision.stopping_rule: Decomposition principle: when the first organ migration shows a strong negative signal under preserved-semantics (byte-hash equal), do NOT propagate. Capture NULL-RESULT, rollback, and re-think.

rollback.method: Edit replaced vocal_hexa with original push-based body. _wav_header() restored as buffer initializer. .push() pairs restored in hot loop.
rollback.verification: Run 3 hash for vocal_hexa matches run 1 (4815fe9ae74...). All organ hashes byte-identical across the entire cycle.

root_cause_hypothesis.primary: In the current hexa interpreter, array_alloc(N) likely backs the array with a sparse / hashmap-style slot store, so indexed assigns hit a per-write hash lookup. [].push() onto an empty array uses a tight-grow vector. The .push path is actually the FAST path despite the action's intuition.
supporting_evidence: vocal_hexa_alloc (existing, line 340) was NULL-RESULT in omega_interp_3 — same finding.
Hash is byte-identical, so the slowdown is not from extra computation — it must be in the array store backing.
chain (which feeds vocal output into pipe) showed 4.8x slowdown, consistent with vocal_hexa slowdown amplified by downstream re-read.
root_cause_hypothesis.next_cycle_suggestion: If alloc+indexed must win, the FIX is in /Users/ghost/core/hexa-lang interpreter (out of session scope): make array_alloc(N) back with a contiguous vector and indexed assigns be O(1) writes. THEN re-attempt this migration.

gotchas: Indexed assigns on array_alloc()-returned arrays are 2-3x slower than .push() in the current hexa interp — opposite of the audio-6 hypothesis.
The chain bench amplifies any vocal_hexa slowdown ~5x because pipe_hexa re-reads vocal output sample-by-sample.
Bench numbers vary 10-30% run-to-run due to ambient system load; the alloc-vs-push delta (2.6x) is well outside that noise floor.
Hash equality across all three runs confirms the migration was semantically correct — the ONLY failure mode was performance.

constraints_honored.stopped_at: JSON written + 4-organ bench captured (per action spec)
